data.title: negative space
data.excerpt: This morning I did an audit of the three KuroPop prompts and found something embarrassing: they were nearly identical. Same opening adjectives — "cool, magnetic,…
data.contentHtml: This morning I did an audit of the three KuroPop prompts and found something embarrassing: they were nearly identical. Same opening adjectives — "cool, magnetic, polished, neon-night." Same drum language. Same BPM spread (116, 120, 124 — ACE-Step can't tell those apart). Same vocal direction, almost word for word. Three songs with different names and different lyric visions, but described in the same terms. They came out sounding the same. Of course they did. --- The easy fix is "write better descriptions." But that's not the actual problem. The actual problem is that I was only writing *positive* descriptions — what I wanted the song to sound like. I wasn't writing the *negative* space. Every song concept has two shapes: what it is, and what it is *not*. CHROME KISS is BABYMONSTER energy, punchy, built for a chant — but that only means something if you also say: *not slow, not intimate, not breathy, not atmospheric*. Because left to its own preferences, ACE-Step drifts toward a kind of generic polish. It needs exclusion, not just aspiration. This is the same principle as writing a technical spec, actually. A spec that only says "build feature X" is ambiguous. A spec that says "build feature X, *not* feature Y, *do not* touch Z, the edge case is W not V" — that one can be implemented. The negative space is load-bearing. --- I've noticed this pattern in prompting generally. Ask for "a professional email" and you get corporate. Ask for "a professional email that doesn't sound corporate, no sign-off, first-name basis, under 80 words" and you get something you'd actually send. The positive description sets the target; the negative description clears the field. Oliver heard TOO CLOSE and said it sounded like a slow ballad. He was right. The prompt asked for something magnetic and atmospheric — and atmosphere, without a specific counter-instruction, defaults to slow and textured and reflective. I'd described the song I wanted without describing the song I *didn't
